name: Goblet Squat
instructions: Hold a dumbbell vertically against your chest with both hands. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out. Lower your hips back and down until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor. Drive through your heels to return to standing. Keep your core braced and chest up throughout.
feelRight: You should feel your quads, glutes, and core working evenly. The movement should feel controlled and upright with the weight staying close to your body.
feelWrong: Leaning forward or letting your knees cave inward puts unnecessary stress on your lower back and joints. Dropping too fast or rounding your lower back at the bottom reduces effectiveness.
notes: Control the descent — don't let gravity do the work. If your heels lift, try a wider stance or work on ankle mobility.

name: Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place one knee and hand on a bench for support with your other foot firmly on the floor. Hold a dumbbell in your free hand with your arm fully extended. Pull the dumbbell toward your hip, driving your elbow back and squeezing your shoulder blade. Lower with control to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel a strong contraction in your lats and mid-back. The motion comes from pulling your elbow back, not twisting your torso.
feelWrong: Rotating your shoulders or using momentum to swing the weight up takes tension off the back muscles. Letting the dumbbell pull your shoulder forward at the bottom reduces range of motion.
notes: Pull the dumbbell toward your hip pocket, not your armpit, to engage your lats more. Keep your neck neutral by looking at the floor.

name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward. Press the dumbbells overhead until your arms are fully extended and the weights are directly over your shoulders. Lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control. Keep your core braced and avoid arching your lower back.
feelRight: You should feel the effort primarily in your deltoids and triceps. The press path should be straight up with minimal forward lean.
feelWrong: Arching your lower back to press the weight shifts load from shoulders to your spine. Flaring your elbows too wide can strain the shoulder joint.
notes: Brace your glutes and abs to protect your lower back. If you can't lock out without arching, the weight is too heavy.

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended and palms facing forward. Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then lower the weights under control. Avoid swinging your body for momentum.
feelRight: You should feel a strong contraction in your biceps at the top of each rep. The movement is isolated to your elbow joint with no shoulder involvement.
feelWrong: Rocking your torso or swinging your elbows forward takes tension off the biceps and can strain your lower back. Dropping the weight too fast on the descent reduces time under tension.
notes: Try a supinated (palms-up) grip for maximum bicep activation. Lower slowly for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
instructions: Set a bench to a 30-45 degree incline and lie back holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level. Press the dumbbells up until your arms are fully extended above your chest. Lower the weights with control until your elbows are just below shoulder level. Keep your shoulders pinned back against the bench.
feelRight: You should feel the work in your upper chest and front delts. The controlled stretch at the bottom followed by a smooth press is the key sensation.
feelWrong: Flaring your elbows out too wide puts excessive strain on the shoulder joint. Bouncing the weights off your chest or using momentum reduces muscle engagement.
notes: Keep your elbows at about a 45-degree angle from your torso. Control the eccentric phase — don't let the weights drop.

name: Dumbbell RDL
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs with feet hip-width apart. Hinge at your hips, pushing them back as you lower the dumbbells down your shins. Keep a flat back and soft knees as you feel a stretch in your hamstrings. Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings and glutes. The hip hinge motion should feel like pushing your butt back toward the wall behind you.
feelWrong: Rounding your lower back to reach the dumbbells further forward shifts the load to your spine. Bending your knees too much turns the exercise into a squat rather than a hamstring-dominant hinge.
notes: Keep the dumbbells close to your legs throughout the movement. Imagine squeezing a pencil between your shoulder blades to maintain upper back tightness.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a slight bend in your elbows. With a neutral grip (palms facing each other), raise the dumbbells out to your sides until they reach shoulder height. Keep your wrists neutral and lead with your elbows. Lower the weights under control.
feelRight: You should feel a burning sensation in your lateral deltoids. Leading with your elbows rather than your hands ensures the shoulders are doing the work.
feelWrong: Shrugging your shoulders up as you lift recruits traps instead of targeting the side delts. Using too much weight forces you to swing or lean back, reducing effectiveness.
notes: Think of pouring a pitcher with your pinkies at the top — slightly tilt the dumbbells forward. Use a lighter weight and focus on strict form for best results.

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level with palms facing forward. Press the dumbbells up until your arms are fully extended above your chest. Lower the weights with control until your elbows are at or just below bench level. Keep your shoulders retracted and feet planted on the floor.
feelRight: You should feel the movement in your chest, front delts, and triceps. The dumbbells should move in a smooth arc, touching at the top.
feelWrong: Letting your shoulders roll forward reduces chest activation and risks impingement. Lowering the dumbbells unevenly or too fast compromises stability.
notes: Squeeze the dumbbells together at the top for better chest contraction. Use a spotter or dump the weights safely if you fail on the last rep.

name: Dumbbell Lunge
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with feet hip-width apart. Step forward with one leg and lower your hips until both knees are bent at about 90 degrees. Your front knee should be above your ankle and your back knee should hover just above the floor. Drive through your front heel to return to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel the exercise in your quads, glutes, and hamstrings. The movement should feel stable and balanced with no twisting or leaning.
feelWrong: Letting your front knee travel past your toes places excessive shear on the knee joint. Leaning forward or taking too short a step reduces glute activation.
notes: Keep your torso upright and core braced throughout. Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side before switching.

name: Dumbbell Shrug
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with arms fully extended. Keeping your arms straight, elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible. Hold the peak contraction for a moment, then lower your shoulders under control. Avoid rolling your shoulders — move straight up and down.
feelRight: You should feel a strong contraction in your upper trapezius. The movement is small and vertical with no engagement from your arms or back.
feelWrong: Rolling your shoulders in circles instead of lifting straight up can cause neck strain. Using momentum or bending your elbows reduces the load on your traps.
notes: Hold at the top for a 1-2 second squeeze for extra trap activation. Keep your chin tucked and neck relaxed throughout.

name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your eyes under the bar and grip it slightly wider than shoulder-width. Unrack the bar and lower it with control to your lower chest or sternum. Press the bar back up explosively while keeping your shoulders retracted and feet flat on the floor. Lock out at the top without flaring your elbows.
feelRight: You should feel a powerful drive through your chest, shoulders, and triceps. The bar path should be a slight curve from your sternum to over your shoulders.
feelWrong: Bouncing the bar off your chest or flaring your elbows to 90 degrees risks injury. Lifting your hips off the bench to press heavier weight reduces stability and effectiveness.
notes: Tuck your elbows at about a 45-degree angle to your torso. Use a spotter for heavy sets and always use the safety bars when training alone.

name: Barbell Back Squat
instructions: Position the bar on your upper back (not your neck) and grip it firmly with hands just outside shoulder width. Unrack and step back, then set your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly out. Descend by bending your knees and hips simultaneously, keeping your chest up and back flat. Drive through your heels to stand back up.
feelRight: You should feel the load distributed across your quads, glutes, and core. The descent and ascent should feel like one smooth motion with the bar traveling in a straight line.
feelWrong: Letting your knees collapse inward or your lower back round at the bottom puts you at serious risk of injury. Lifting your heels off the ground indicates poor ankle mobility or stance width.
notes: Take a big breath and brace your core before each rep. If you can't hit depth without good form, reduce the weight or work on mobility.

name: Barbell Deadlift
instructions: Stand with your feet hip-width apart and the bar over the middle of your feet. Hinge at your hips and bend your knees to grip the bar just outside your shins. With a flat back and chest up, drive through your whole foot to pull the bar up close to your body. Lock out at the top by squeezing your glutes, then lower the bar with control.
feelRight: You should feel the lift in your hamstrings, glutes, spinal erectors, and grip. The bar should stay in contact with your legs throughout the pull.
feelWrong: Rounding your lower back to reach the bar places dangerous shear forces on your spine. Jerking the bar off the floor or hyperextending at the top wastes energy and increases injury risk.
notes: Always reset your back position and brace before each rep, not just the first one. Use a mixed or hook grip for heavier loads to improve security.

name: Barbell Overhead Press
instructions: Rack the bar at shoulder height and grip it just outside shoulder width. Step back and brace your core and glutes tightly. Press the bar overhead in a straight line until your arms are fully locked out and the bar is directly over your head. Lower the bar back to your shoulders with control.
feelRight: You should feel the effort in your deltoids, triceps, and upper chest. The bar should travel straight up with your head moving back slightly to clear the way.
feelWrong: Arching your lower back to push the bar overhead shifts load to your spine instead of your shoulders. Flaring your elbows too wide can cause impingement.
notes: Squeeze your glutes and abs throughout to protect your lower back. If the bar drifts forward of your midline, you're likely using too much weight.

name: Barbell Row
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip just outside your legs. Hinge forward at your hips until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to the floor. Pull the bar toward your lower ribcage by driving your elbows back, squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower the bar under control to full arm extension.
feelRight: You should feel a strong contraction in your lats, rhomboids, and traps. Your torso should stay stable with no excessive bobbing or twisting.
feelWrong: Using your lower back to yank the bar up turns the row into a bad deadlift. Letting your shoulders round forward at the bottom disengages the lats completely.
notes: Keep your head in line with your spine — don't look up. Imagine pulling the bar apart as you row for greater lat activation.

name: Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip, hands shoulder-width apart and arms fully extended. Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, curl the bar toward your shoulders by flexing your elbows. Squeeze your biceps at the top, then lower the bar under control. Avoid swinging your body for momentum.
feelRight: You should feel a deep contraction in both biceps heads. The bar should move smoothly through an arc with no shoulder or back involvement.
feelWrong: Rocking your torso or flaring your elbows forward to heave the weight up takes tension off the biceps. Lowering the bar too fast removes the eccentric stimulus critical for growth.
notes: Use an EZ-bar if a straight bar causes wrist discomfort. Pause at the bottom for a second to eliminate momentum on each rep.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench and a barbell over your hips. Roll the bar into your hip crease and grip it with your hands on either side. Drive through your heels to lift your hips until your thighs are parallel to the floor. Squeeze your glutes hard at the top, then lower with control.
feelRight: You should feel an intense contraction in your glutes at the top of the movement. Your abs and lower back should stay neutral without overextending.
feelWrong: Hyperextending your lower back at the top shifts work from glutes to spinal erectors. Letting your feet slide or not driving through your heels reduces hip drive.
notes: Use a pad or folded mat under the bar to protect your hip bones. Tuck your chin and keep your ribs down to avoid overarching.

name: Dumbbell Skullcrusher
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a pair of dumbbells straight up over your chest, palms facing each other. Keeping your upper arms strictly stationary and perpendicular to the floor, bend your elbows to lower the dumbbells down toward the sides of your head. Pause when you feel a stretch in your triceps, then contract your triceps to extend your arms back to the starting position.
feelRight: You should feel a strong stretch and subsequent contraction entirely in the back of your upper arms (triceps). Your elbows should remain pointed at the ceiling, functioning purely as a hinge.
feelWrong: Allowing your upper arms to move back and forth turns the movement into a pullover, engaging the lats and chest rather than isolating the triceps. Flaring your elbows excessively outward can place undue stress on the elbow joint.
notes: Start with light weights to master the form and ensure elbow comfort. Lowering the dumbbells slightly behind the head rather than directly beside the ears can increase the stretch on the long head of the triceps.
